Bibliography

  • Miller-Dervant Catalog, 1906, Miller-Dervant Company Records, 1896–1955, MsSC 198, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.

  • Nieuwe Groninger Courant, dag ed., March 15, 1891, Groninger Archieven, PPN 440645174, accessed December 3, 2025, https://www.delpher.nl/.

  • Oregonian (Portland, OR), “A Big Dutch Colony: Two Hundred Families Will Come to Washington Next Summer,” December 28, 1894.
  • Raymer’s Dictionary of Spokane and the Inland Empire: A Complete Encyclopaedic Dictionary with Maps, Street Guides, Illustrations, etc. (Spokane: [Raymer’s], 1906), Inland Northwest Special Collections, Spokane Public Library, Spokane, WA.
  • Rocky Mountain Husbandman (Townsend, MT), December 4, 1890, 3.
  • Songs as Sung at the Dutch Mill of the Coeur d’Alene Hotel in Spokane, Jerome Peltier Archive of Spokane Area History, 1934–2004, MsSC 5753, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • Spokane in 1897, July 1956, Jerome Peltier Archive of Spokane Area History, 1934–2004, MsSC 5753, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • Spokane, The City Beautiful: A Souvenir of Spokane, Washington, and the Inland Empire, n.d., Inland Northwest Special Collections, Spokane Public Library, Spokane, WA.
  • Spokane, the Queen of the Inland Empire (1902), Inland Northwest Special Collections, Spokane Public Library, Spokane, WA.
  • Sutton Family Papers, ca. 1884–ca. 1910, MsSC 131, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • The Colville Examiner, no. 317 (November 22, 1913).
  • The Henderson-Ames Co. Catalogue, ca. 1900s, Eastern Washington University Archives, John F. Kennedy Memorial Library, Cheney, WA.
  • The Northwest Magazine, April 1890, 14.
  • The Northwest Magazine, “Chicago’s Great Fire of 1871,” April 1890, 14.
  • The Orator, December 23, 1905, Pearl Hutton Shrader Papers, 1897–1943, MsSC 151, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • The Western Architect (1908), Inland Empire Architect Collection, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • Wall Papers; Artistic Wall Hangings, n.d., John W. Graham & Co., Andrew T. Amos Papers, 1904–1911, MsSC 11, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • A. Rutgers Van der Loeff Diaries, 1893?–1916, MsSC 125, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • Adam H. Irving Papers, 1905-1943. MsSC 141. Eastern Washington State Historical Society (Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture). Spokane, WA.
  • Augustus J. Veenendaal, Slow Train to Paradise: How Dutch Investment Helped Build American Railroads (Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press, 1996).
  • Blackface minstrel shows have been defined as “a form of expropriation, by which Black people were divested of their own cultural representation, through the business of staging or manufacturing race.” Eric Lott, "‘The Seeming Counterfeit’: Racial Politics and Early Blackface Minstrelsy," American Quarterly 43, no. 2 (1991): 223–54, https://doi.org/10.2307/2712925.
  • C. Lance Brockman, Kenneth L. Ames, and Susan C. Jones, Theatre of the Fraternity: Staging the Ritual Space of the Scottish Rite of Freemasonry, 1896–1929 (Minneapolis: Frederick R. Weisman Art Museum, University of Minnesota; distributed by University Press of Mississippi, 1996).
  • Carl Dahlgren, Bird’s Eye View of Spokane, ca. 1880s, in The Illustrated History of Washington Territory and Valley of the Columbia, Ephemera Collection, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture (Eastern Washington State Historical Society), Spokane, WA.
  • Charles A. (Charles August) Libby, Spokane – Parades (#04), photograph, August 1936, “Parade showing wagons, streetcars and buses,” Northwest Room, Spokane Public Library, accessed January 24, 2026, https://lange.spokanelibrary.org/items/show/2808.
  • Charles Evans Hughes, “The Monroe Doctrine after 100 Years,” Current History 19, no. 1 (October 1923): 102–13, https://www.jstor.org/stable/45329537.
  • Charles Woodruff Clarke Papers, 1866–1933, Ms 27, Eastern Washington State Historical Society, Northwest Museum of Arts and Culture, Spokane, WA.
  • Doris J. Woodward, Harper Joy: Spokane’s Man of Many Faces: Let the Show Begin (Spokane, WA: Tornado Creek Publications, 2007).
  • E.T. Hawk Photograph Collection, SPC-023-0996, item SPC-023-0996_02_266, Eastern Washington University Archives, John F. Kennedy Memorial Library, Cheney, WA.
  • E.T. Hawk Photograph Collection. SPC-023-0996. Item SPC-023-0996_03_407. Eastern Washington University Archives, John F. Kennedy Memorial Library, Cheney, WA.
  • Edgar C. Duin, “Settlers’ Periodical: Eugene Smalley and the Northwest Magazine,” Minnesota History 33, no. 1 (1952): 29–34.
  • Elizabeth C. Cromley and Carter L. Hudgins, Gender, Class, and Shelter (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1995).
  • Elizabeth C. Cromley and Carter L. Hudgins, Gender, Class, and Shelter, 1st ed., Perspectives in Vernacular Architecture (Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1995).
  • Financien en Economie. Binnenland., De Maasbode, vol. 55, no. 18775 (May 24, 1923, evening edition).
  • Frank Mehring, “Deutsch, Dutch, Double Dutch: Authentic and Artificial German-American Dialects,” Amerikastudien / American Studies 51, no. 1 (2006): 93–113.
  • Hal K. Rothman, “Selling the Meaning of Place: Entrepreneurship, Tourism, and Community Transformation in the Twentieth-Century American West,” Pacific Historical Review 65, no. 4 (1996): 525–57.
  • Henry Stephen Lucas, Netherlanders in America: Dutch Immigration to the United States and Canada, 1789–1950, University of Michigan Publications, History and Political Science, vol. 21 (Ann Arbor: University of Michigan Press, 1955).
  • Hoefer, F. A., and J. van Hinte. Historical Atlas of the Netherlands. ’s-Gravenhage: Nijhoff, 1934.
  • J. P. Niermeyer, “Eerste Blad. Indrukken van Amerika VII: Het ‘Inland Empire,’” Het Nieuws van den Dag: Kleine Courant (Amsterdam), no. 13230, January 27, 1913.
  • Jacob van Hinte, Netherlanders in America: A Study of Emigration and Settlement in the Nineteenth and Twentieth Centuries in the United States of America (Grand Rapids, MI: Baker Book House, 1985; originally published 1928).
  • Jacob van Hinte, Netherlanders in America: A Study of Emigration and Settlement in the Nineteenth and Twentieth Centuries in the United States of America, vol. 2 (Grand Rapids, MI: Baker Book House, 1985).
  • Jennifer M. Black, “Exchange Cards: Advertising, Album Making, and the Commodification of Sentiment in the Gilded Age,” Winterthur Portfolio 51, no. 1 (2017): 1–53, https://doi.org/10.1086/693448.
  • John Fahey, “Irrigation, Apples, and the Spokane Country,” The Pacific Northwest Quarterly 84, no. 1 (January 1993): 7–18.
  • John Fahey, “When the Dutch Owned Spokane,” Pacific Northwest Quarterly 72, no. 1 (1981): 2-10.
  • Juan R. Torruella, "Ruling America's Colonies: The 'Insular Cases,'" Yale Law & Policy Review 32, no. 1 (2013): 57-95.
  • Kate Howk, “The Social Nucleus of a Small Town: A Case for the Restoration of the Bovill Opera House,” The Pacific Northwest Quarterly 114, no. 4 (2023): 142–57.
  • Katherine G. Morrissey, “Miss Spokane and the Inland Northwest: Representations of Regions and Gender,” Frontiers: A Journal of Women Studies 22, no. 3 (2001): 6–12, https://doi.org/10.2307/3347234.
  • Koers van het Geld, Algemeen Handelsblad, vol. 69, no. 21270 (August 22, 1896, evening edition).
  • Leigh H. (Leigh Hadley) Irvine, A History of the New California, Its Resources and People;, with Allen County Public Library Genealogy Center (New York, Chicago, The Lewis publishing company, 1905), http://archive.org/details/historyofnewcali00irvi.
  • M. M. Manring, Slave in a Box: The Strange Career of Aunt Jemima, The American South Series (Charlottesville: University of Virginia Press, 1998).
  • Manissa M. Maharawal and Erin McElroy, “The Anti-Eviction Mapping Project: Counter Mapping and Oral History toward Bay Area Housing Justice,” Annals of the American Association of Geographers 108, no. 2 (2018): 380–89.
  • Marilyn Maness Mehaffy, “Advertising Race/Raceing Advertising: The Feminine Consumer(-Nation), 1876-1900,” Signs: Journal of Women in Culture and Society 23, no. 1 (1997): 131–74, https://doi.org/10.1086/495238.
  • Michelle M. Murdock, “Polishing Up the Diamond Trade: How to Revitalize the Kimberley Process,” Journal of Corporation Law 46, no. 2 (2020): 463–81.
  • Michelle W. Lewis, “Perspectives on Predatory Lending: The Philadelphia Experience,” Journal of Affordable Housing & Community Development Law 12, no. 4 (2003): 491–520.
  • Mira Wilkins, “Dutch Multinational Enterprises in the United States: A Historical Summary,” Business History Review 79, no. 2 (2005): 193–273.
  • Paula Becker, “Marguerite Motie Becomes the First Miss Spokane on March 29, 1912,” HistoryLink.org, Essay 9104, August 9, 2009, accessed December 3, 2025, https://www.historylink.org/File/9104.
  • Robert L. Beisner, “Thirty Years Before Manila: E. L. Godkin, Carl Schurz, and Anti-Imperialism in the Gilded Age,” The Historian 30, no. 4 (August 1968): 561–77, contributed by Taylor & Francis, Ltd., https://www.jstor.org/stable/24440884.
  • Robert P. Swierenga, “Jacob Van Hinte: Sociographer and Historian of Dutch-American Immigration and Settlement,” paper presented at the biennial conference of the Association for the Advancement of Dutch-American Studies, Central College, Pella, IA, October 8, 1981.
  • Sanford J. Ungar, “Special Interests: Is US Foreign Policy for Sale?” Great Decisions (1998): 9–18.
  • Saskia Coenen Snyder, “‘As Long as It Sparkles!’: The Diamond Industry in Nineteenth-Century Amsterdam,” Jewish Social Studies 22, no. 2 (2017): 38–73, https://doi.org/10.2979/jewisocistud.22.2.02.
  • Saskia Coenen Snyder, “‘As Long as It Sparkles!’: The Diamond Industry in Nineteenth-Century Amsterdam,” Jewish Social Studies 22, no. 2 (2017): 38–73.
  • Steve Glischinski, “Making Great Northern Great,” Trains 79, no. 1 (January 2019): 36–43.
  • Susan B. Matheson, “The Goddess Tyche,” Yale University Art Gallery Bulletin (1994): 18–33.
  • Susan E. Gray, “Limits and Possibilities: White-Indian Relations in Western Michigan in the Era of Removal,” Michigan Historical Review 20, no. 2 (1994): 71–91.
  • William Cronon, Nature’s Metropolis: Chicago and the Great West, Norton Paperback (New York: W. W. Norton & Company, 1992).
  • William D. Moore, “Riding the Goat: Secrecy, Masculinity, and Fraternal High Jinks in the United States, 1845–1930,” Winterthur Portfolio 41, no. 2/3 (2007): 161–88, https://doi.org/10.1086/518920.
